title: Amniotic Fluid Volume
authors: Taylor KH, Simmons PM, Magann EF / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Amniotic Fluid Volume?
น้ำคร่ำ (amniotic fluid) ไม่ใช่แค่ 'น้ำที่ล้อมรอบทารก' — มันเป็น indirect mirror of fetal well-being. (1) Oligohydramnios → pulmonary hypoplasia, cord accidents, IUGR — PNM rate 11% vs 0.2% in normal AFV (Chamberlain 1984); (2) Polyhydramnios → congenital anomalies (esophageal/duodenal atresia), diabetes, preterm birth, cord prolapse; (3) AFV assessment ด้วย AFI vs SDP ต่างกันมากในอัตรา intervention: AFI over-diagnoses oligohydramnios 2x vs SDP → more CS/induction โดยไม่ improve outcome (Magann 2004); (4) หลัง PPROM <26 wk: ถ้า SDP <2 cm → pulmonary hypoplasia 70%; ถ้า SDP ≥2 cm → survival 98%; (5) indomethacin ลด AFV แต่เสี่ยง ductal constriction 50% หลัง 32 wk — ต้อง stop ก่อน 32 wk. เราเป็นทีมที่รับทารกเหล่านี้ — ต้องรู้ว่า obstetric decision นั้นใช้ AFI หรือ SDP และมีผลต่อทารกอย่างไร.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย amniotic fluid dynamics: pathway production/removal (urine, lung fluid, swallowing, intramembranous, transmembranous) พร้อม volume ต่อวัน
เปรียบเทียบ AFI vs SDP vs subjective estimation: วิธีวัด, ค่า cut-off, ข้อดีข้อเสีย, accuracy เทียบกับ dye-dilution technique
ระบุ normal AFV ตาม gestational age: peak ที่ GA ใด, ค่าปกติ AFI/SDP ที่ 37–41 wk
วิเคราะห์ oligohydramnios: definition, etiology (PROM/renal/placental/medications), diagnostic workup (speculum/ferning/PAMG-1/anatomy/Doppler/growth)
ระบุ pulmonary hypoplasia risk ใน oligohydramnios: window of vulnerability (16–26 wk), SDP cut-off, sonographic prediction (lung area ratio, thoracic/abdominal ratio)
อธิบาย treatment oligohydramnios: hydration, amnioinfusion, tissue sealants — evidence level แต่ละอย่าง
วิเคราะห์ polyhydramnios: definition, etiology (swallowing defect/diabetes/chromosomal/idiopathic), prevalence anomaly ตาม severity
ระบุ treatment polyhydramnios: amnioreduction, indomethacin (dose/risk/gestational limit), sulindac
อธิบาย twin pregnancy fluid assessment: SDP preference, TOPS/TTTS diagnosis, surveillance frequency
เชื่อม amniotic fluid abnormality กับ neonatal outcomes ที่ต้องเตรียมรับ: pulmonary hypoplasia, cord accident, preterm birth, GI anomaly
1. Amniotic Fluid Dynamics
1.1 Composition
98–99% water; solute composition เปลี่ยนตาม GA
ไตรมาส 1: osmolarity ≈ maternal plasma (~290 mOsm/kg) — amniotic fluid เป็น transudate จาก maternal plasma ผ่าน placental surface หรือ fetal skin
Fetal skin keratinizes at 22–25 wk: ก่อน 22–25 wk skin ทำหน้าที่เป็น membrane ให้ fluid ผ่าน freely; หลังจากนั้น hypotonic fetal urine ↓ osmolality จาก 290 → ~255 mOsm/kg near term
ปลายไตรมาส 3: ↑ creatinine/urea/uric acid (จาก fetal urine); ↑ particulate matter (vernix, lanugo, desquamated cells, meconium)
Protein (primarily albumin): low concentration in late pregnancy — minor fetal nutrition source
1.2 Production and Removal Pathways

Amniotic Fluid Production/Removal — Volumes per Day
Production: Fetal urine = 800–1200 mL/day (หลัก); lung fluid entering AF = ~150–200 mL/day (half of 300–400 mL secreted, half swallowed at trachea). Removal: Swallowing = 500–1000 mL/day (หลัก); intramembranous reabsorption (amnion → fetal blood via aquaporins) = 200–500 mL/day. Transmembranous movement (maternal circulation ↔ amnion via placenta): ปริมาณน้อยมาก — ไม่ใช่ major contributor. Aquaporins: small 26–34 kDa membrane proteins ใน chorioamniotic membrane + placenta — regulate water flux; adaptive to abnormal AFV; may be future therapeutic target.
Fetal urine production: เริ่มที่ 8–11 wk; 3D bladder volume measurement → 7–70 mL/h (24 wk ถึง delivery). ↓ ใน placental insufficiency + IUGR
Fetal swallowing: เริ่มที่ 8–11 wk; near-term 210–840 mL/day (mean 565 mL) ≈ 5–10% fetal body weight. ↑ volume ตาม GA
Key: renal agenesis → virtual anhydramnios; esophageal obstruction → polyhydramnios — สาเหตุตรงข้ามกัน
Lung fluid: chloride ion exchange drives 300–400 mL/day secretion; half enters AF, half swallowed; larynx ปิดป้องกัน AF เข้า lung
1.3 Meconium Staining
Rate: 7–27% ขึ้นกับ population; meconium aspiration ผ่าน trachea: <1–5% และมักมี neonatal hypoxia ร่วม
Rarely: aspiration of meconium-free AF reported as cause of neonatal death (autopsy-confirmed)
2. Measurement of Amniotic Fluid Volume
2.1 Gold Standards (Impractical Clinically)
Direct measurement at CS: accurate แต่ single occasion only; accuracy vs dye-dilution: 7% difference (r=0.99, P<.001)
Dye-dilution (para-amino hippurate): invasive (amniocentesis needed) + lab support required → impractical for serial assessment
MRI: expensive + impractical; not standard-of-care
2.2 Sonographic Techniques — Clinical Use

AFI vs SDP vs Subjective — สรุปเปรียบเทียบ
AFI (Amniotic Fluid Index): sum of largest vertical pocket ใน 4 quadrants (umbilicus = center divider; linea nigra = L/R divider); transducer perpendicular to floor; 1-cm horizontal minimum. Phelan 1987. SDP (Single Deepest Pocket): ตั้งแต่ Chamberlain 1984; largest vertical pocket globally; 1-cm horizontal minimum. Key finding: AFI over-diagnoses oligohydramnios 2x more than SDP — double the inductions + CS for fetal distress (30% vs 15%; 13% vs 7%) with NO improvement in perinatal outcome (Magann 2004; Chauhan 2004; Cochrane 2009). Accuracy of all 3 techniques: 65–70% for normal; all POOR for abnormal volumes. Twin: summated AFI poorly predicts dye-determined oligo/polyhydramnios in twins.
2.3 Technical Notes
Probe perpendicular to floor vs perpendicular to uterine contour: correlation coefficient 0.82 (AFI), 0.7 (SDP) — both acceptable (Hughes 2019)
Color Doppler NOT helpful: over-diagnosed oligohydramnios in 37% of women with normal dye-determined AFV (Magann 2001)
3D US: promising but not yet validated against gold standard for AFV assessment
Clinical takeaway: SDP preferred for antenatal testing — Cochrane 2009 (Nabhan & Abdelmoula): SDP superior to AFI for antenatal testing (AFI → more interventions, no better outcome)
3. Normal Amniotic Fluid Volume — Singleton
3.1 Volume Trajectory Across Gestation
Gravid women: ~6 L total extra fluid during pregnancy — 2800 mL fetus, 400 mL placenta, 700–800 mL amniotic fluid; remainder: maternal uterus/breasts/blood
AFV trajectory (dye-determined): ↑ steadily early gestation → stable 22–38 wk (mean ~750–800 mL) → ↓ late 3rd trimester
After 33–34 wk: ↓ ~8% per week; mean ~500 mL at 40–42 wk
Mean AFI: ~12–14 cm throughout most of pregnancy; ↓ after 33 wk; 95th percentile ≈ 20 cm; absolute max normal <24–25 cm
Quantile regression (preferred statistical method): gestational age-specific reference intervals — 1190 normal AFVs (Ounpraseuth J Obstet Gynaecol Res 2017)
5th percentile cutoff vs AFI <5: Shanks 2011 (15,000 patients): AFI <5th perc better predicts NICU admission than fixed cutoff <5 cm
4. Amniotic Fluid in Twin Pregnancies
4.1 Assessment in Twins
SDP is preferred method in twins: Morin & Lim (SOGC): SDP with singleton cut-offs; summated AFI poorly predicts dye-determined oligo/polyhydramnios in twins
Dye-dilution in 3rd trimester diamniotic twins: AFV per sac ≈ normal singleton → singleton normograms reasonable
Diamniotic twin SDP: constant 17–37 wk (Magann 2007); suggested cut-offs: <2.2 cm = low; >7.5 cm = high for adverse peripartum/neonatal outcomes
4.2 Polyhydramnios in Twins
Hernandez 2012 (1951 twin pregnancies, TTTS excluded): polyhydramnios in 18% — mild (SDP 8–9.9), moderate (10–11.9), severe (≥12 cm)
Severe polyhydramnios: ↑ major anomalies (prevalence ~20%); associated with stillbirth in monochorionic pregnancies (27%, P<.001)
Not associated with: preterm delivery, FGR, NICU admission, neonatal death — except for anomalies
4.3 TOPS and TTTS

Twin Oligohydramnios-Polyhydramnios Sequence (TOPS) และ TTTS
TOPS: discordant AFV in monochorionic twins (TTTS = extreme endpoint). TTTS: 3–5x ↑ mortality/morbidity; discordant fetal weight + AFV. Sonographic signs: 'stuck twin' (donor sac — tightly adherent membranes wrap the fetus like plastic wrap) + polyhydramnios (recipient sac). Surveillance: every 2 wk from 2nd trimester if discordant; every 2 wk for concordant MC/DA. Treatment: laser therapy ↑ frequency for MC/DA TTTS; refer to MFM.
5. Oligohydramnios
5.1 Definition
AFV <200–500 mL (direct measurement); occurs in 1–2% pregnancies
Sonographic definition: SDP <2 cm OR AFI <5 cm OR subjectively low
Borderline fluid: AFI 5–8 cm (or 5–10 cm by some) — associated with fetal malformations if diagnosed at 24–34 wk (Magann 2011)
PNM rate: 11% in oligohydramnios vs 0.2% in normal AFV (Chamberlain 1984); borderline SDP 1–2 cm: PNM 3.7%
5.2 Etiology

rows: Renal agenesis/bilateral multicystic dysplastic kidney → virtual anhydramnios from 16 wk; urinary tract obstruction (posterior urethral valves = 'keyhole' bladder on US); chromosomal (T18, T21); IUGR; post-term; IUFD
Most common cause: 50% of oligohydramnios cases (Shenker 1991); occult or overt; preterm or prolonged
Uteroplacental insufficiency; autoimmune (APS); hypertension; nephropathy; diabetic vasculopathy; preeclampsia; placental infarction; chronic abruption; TTTS (donor)

Prostaglandin synthetase inhibitors (indomethacin, ibuprofen): ↓ GFR → ↓ fetal urine output (70% patients); ACE inhibitors: severe fetal hypotension → anuria, renal anomalies, skull ossification defects, death — CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy
Remaining cases without identifiable cause — responsive to maternal hydration

blocks.text: 5.3 Clinical Implications
↑ IUGR (BW <10th perc or AC <2.5th perc); ↑ meconium-stained AF; ↑ fetal labor intolerance; ↓ Apgar scores
Cord accidents, fetal lung hypoplasia, contractures (1st–2nd trimester) — mechanical compression + lack of fluid for movement/growth

Controversial: in 'isolated oligohydramnios' (normal anatomy, no PROM), greatest risk may be iatrogenic prematurity from rush to delivery (Melamed 2011). Low AFV + IUGR: OR 11.1 for NICU admission but no other independent morbidity identified (Magann 2011)
5.4 Diagnostic Workup
Step 1: Rule Out PROM
Sterile speculum exam: pooling of AF in posterior vagina → diagnosis confirmed
Nitrazine paper: alkaline pH of AF (turns blue); ferning: NaCl in AF crystallizes; beware cervical mucus artifact
AmniSure (PAMG-1 monoclonal antibody test): sensitivity 98.7%, specificity 87.5%; false-positive with bleeding; false-negative if >12h after rupture
Indigo carmine amniocentesis + vaginal tampon: confirms PROM in equivocal cases (methylene blue: causes methemoglobinemia → NOT used)
PPROM before 24 wk: SDP >1 cm → 60% live birth vs SDP <1 cm → 8% (Storness-Bliss 2012)
Step 2: Assess Fetal Anatomy
Full anatomic survey: renal parenchyma, renal pelvis, bladder morphology (keyhole = posterior urethral valves)
Bilateral renal agenesis: anhydramnios from 16 wk; adrenals hypertrophy mimic kidneys → Doppler to confirm absent renal arteries
Coexisting: CHD, CNS anomalies; chromosomal (T18, T21 with renal anomalies) → cfDNA or genetic amniocentesis
Transabdominal amnioinfusion (saline) as adjunct for anatomy visualization in anhydramnios: suspected anomaly confirmed 90%; dx changed 13% (Fisk 1991)
Step 3: Assess Fetal Growth + Doppler
Uteroplacental insufficiency → asymmetric IUGR: small AC, head-sparing. Umbilical artery AEDV/REDV → ↑ PNM → indication for intensive monitoring ± delivery
MCA Doppler: ↑ diastolic flow = fetal hypoxia + cerebral vasodilation — late compensatory finding
Abnormal ductus venosus flow + MCA pulsatility: late signs of fetal compromise → delivery consideration
Step 4: Assess Pulmonary Status

Pulmonary Hypoplasia — Critical Window 16–26 Weeks
Risk greatest if oligohydramnios during canalicular phase (16–26 wk): alveolar proliferation depends on fluid distension of airways + trophic function. Survival: 30% if SDP <2 cm vs 98% if SDP ≥2 cm (20–25 wk), Vergani 1994. Probability of pulmonary hypoplasia ↑ steeply if PPROM at <25 wk (50% probability at ~25 wk). Sonographic predictors: lung area ratio = (chest area − cardiac area)/chest area × 100; normal >66% (heart <1/3 of chest). Sensitivity 85%, specificity 85% (Vintzileos 1989). Thoracic/abdominal ratio <0.08 → virtually 100% predictive lethal pulmonary hypoplasia (D'Alton 1992). 3D lung volumes: sensitivity 94%, specificity 82%.
6. Treatment of Oligohydramnios
6.1 Term Oligohydramnios
Induction vs expectant management (≥40 wk): single RCT n=54 (Ek 2005): no difference in delivery mode, Apgar, cord pH
MFM survey (n>500): 92% would recommend induction without documented lung maturity <39 wk; 35% before 37 wk — but only 1/3 felt induction decreases adverse outcomes. No consensus.
Expectant management: Doppler monitoring if placental insufficiency suspected; encourage consistent oral hydration
6.2 PROM Management
Evidence of infection → deliver; otherwise expectant hospitalization until 34 wk or abnormal antenatal testing
Antibiotics to prolong latency; corticosteroids for lung maturity; most deliver within 1 wk
6.3 Maternal Hydration
Idiopathic oligohydramnios: responsive to hydration (oral = IV; no long-term difference) — Magann 2003; Patrelli 2012
Effect short-lived without continued hydration; left-lateral positioning ↑ AFI
Clinical application limited: helps in scenarios where fluid assists outcome (external cephalic version, amniocentesis) — NOT a definitive treatment
6.4 Amnioinfusion
Prophylactic transcervical amnioinfusion (term, oligohydramnios induced): ↓ CS rate — reduces cord compression during labor
Complications: uterine overdistention, hypertonus, infection, amniotic fluid embolism — use with cautious monitoring
Amnioinfusion for meconium-stained AF: NO LONGER RECOMMENDED
Serial transabdominal amnioinfusion (2nd trimester PPROM <26 wk): maintain SDP >2 cm → ↓ pulmonary hypoplasia 62% → 10%; ↓ abnormal neurology (Locatelli 2000, n=49) — RAFT trial ongoing
6.5 Medications Causing Oligohydramnios

Indomethacin-Induced Oligohydramnios — Risk Management
Mechanism: prostaglandin synthetase inhibition → ↓ fetal GFR → ↓ fetal urine output (up to 70% patients). Also: ↓ uteroplacental perfusion + premature ductal closure. Reversible if stopped early. Monitoring rule: if indomethacin >72 h → semiweekly AFV assessment + fetal echo (ductal flow). ACE inhibitors: severe fetal hypotension → ↓ renal blood flow → prolonged neonatal anuria, renal anomalies, skull ossification defects, death. CONTRAINDICATED throughout pregnancy.
7. Polyhydramnios
7.1 Definition
AFV >2000 mL (direct measurement); 1–3% of pregnancies
Sonographic: SDP >8 cm OR AFI >24–25 cm OR AFV >95th percentile OR subjectively high
Severity: mild (AFI 24–40 cm or SDP 8–9.9), moderate (SDP 10–11.9), severe (SDP ≥12 cm or AFI >40 cm)
Presentation: ↑ fundal height, dyspnea, edema, ↑ weight
7.2 Etiology

Polyhydramnios Etiologies — Prevalence Pattern
50–60% idiopathic (most common overall). Congenital anomalies (impaired swallowing/absorption): prevalence anomaly correlates with severity: mild polyhydramnios: 29% anomaly; severe polyhydramnios: 75% anomaly (2.6x higher). Maternal diabetes: ~15% of polyhydramnios (gestational + pregestational → ↑ fetal urination from hyperglycemia). Chromosomal: 3–13% of polyhydramnios. Aneuploidy: isolated polyhydramnios ≠ strong predictor of gestational diabetes; but if macrosomia → screen with GCT.

rows: Esophageal atresia (absent stomach bubble); tracheoesophageal fistula; duodenal atresia (double bubble); diaphragmatic hernia; bowel obstruction; anencephaly; trisomy 18/21; muscular dystrophy; fetal akinesia; skeletal dysplasias; micrognathia; cleft palate
Maternal diabetes (↑ fetal urination from hyperglycemia); constitutional macrosomia
Immune hydrops (Rh isoimmunization); nonimmune hydrops; fetal anemia (parvovirus, thalassemia, fetomaternal hemorrhage)
Arrhythmias; teratomas; hemangiomas; myotonic dystrophy; Pena-Shokeir

blocks.text: 7.3 Clinical Implications
Retrospective reviews: association with macrosomia, preterm birth, nonreactive NST, perinatal morbidity, congenital anomalies
'Idiopathic' polyhydramnios: 2–5x ↑ PNM risk (Magann 2007); postnatal: 11/118 had undetected GI anomalies (Abele 2012)
Neonatal risks: respiratory distress, hypoglycemia (even without identified anomaly); macrosomia complications
High risk: PROM, cord prolapse, preterm uterine contractions (overdistended uterus)
7.4 Diagnostic Workup
Detailed anatomic US: GI system — absent stomach bubble (proximal obstruction: esophageal atresia/TEF); 'double bubble' (duodenal atresia)
Hydrops fetalis signs: ascites, pleural effusion, pericardial effusion, skin edema — polyhydramnios may be first presenting sign
Diabetes screen: GCT/OGTT especially if macrosomia or asymmetric ↑ fetal AC
Infectious workup: syphilis, Rh isoimmunization, atypical antibodies → hemolytic disease; recent exposure history → TORCH
Genetic: aneuploidy evaluation if markers present or severe polyhydramnios + IUGR (↑ PNM risk; Erez 2005)
7.5 Treatment
Mild Idiopathic Polyhydramnios (AFI 24–40 cm)
Perinatal outcome similar to normal AFV except ↑ LGA (>4000g). No intervention required beyond monitoring
Antenatal testing: start at 24 wk (no consensus on type or interval); watch for cervical change if contracting
Severe Polyhydramnios (AFI >40 cm)
Consider amnioreduction or indomethacin for significant maternal complications (dyspnea, overdistension)
Amnioreduction
Mechanism: removes excess fluid → ↓ uterine baseline tonus + contractility (Calderyo-Barcia 1957)
Serial amnioreduction usually needed; Leung 2004 (134 procedures, 74 patients; max vertical pocket >10 cm): 64% needed repeat; 70% without TTTS needed only 1 procedure
Complications: PROM, preterm labor, abruption, fetal bradycardia, infection, maternal hypoproteinemia
Indomethacin

Indomethacin ใน Polyhydramnios — Dose, Effect, Risk
Mechanism: ↑ fetal vasopressin release → antidiuretic effect → ↓ fetal urine; ↑ fluid reabsorption by fetal lungs; ↑ transmembranous AF absorption. Dose: 25 mg orally q6h (up to 100 mg q4–8h depending on severity). Stop when AFV ↓ by 1/3 to 1/2 or AFI <20 cm. Effective in >90% of polyhydramnios cases. Risks: premature ductal closure (5% at 27 wk → ~50% at 32 wk); renal complications; NEC. MUST stop before 32 wk. Total dose <200 mg/day; duration <48h reduces complications. Sulindac: less ductal closure than indomethacin (purported) — not prospectively studied for polyhydramnios.
8. Management of Multifetal Pregnancies
Dichorionic/diamniotic: same as singleton; abnormal fluid → look for TTTS, IUGR, anomalies
Monochorionic/diamniotic TOPS or TTTS: refer to MFM; laser therapy with ↑ frequency; AFV assessment every 2 wk from 16 wk
Monoamniotic twins: abnormal AFV does NOT alter management — antenatal testing from viability; deliver early by plan
General twin surveillance: AFV assessment + growth scans every 4 wk
Bilateral polyhydramnios (twin or shared amnion): may need fluid reduction for maternal comfort; ↑ preterm labor
9. Algorithm — Diagnostic Approach to Oligohydramnios

rows: Use SDP (preferred). Subjective assessment as adjunct
Sterile speculum (pooling, nitrazine, ferning); AmniSure if equivocal; if SDP <1 cm + 16–26 wk → very high PROM probability
Renal/urinary system (renal agenesis → Doppler RA); bladder morphology; CHD/CNS/skeletal co-anomalies; chromosomal evaluation
EFW, AC, HC; UA Doppler (AEDV/REDV = intensive monitoring); MCA + DV if UA abnormal
GA at onset; SDP ≥2 cm at 20–25 wk → favorable. Lung area ratio, thoracic/abdominal circumference ratio
PROM: expectant/ABx/steroids; IUGR: Doppler + timing; idiopathic: hydration; term: induction vs expectant

blocks.text: 10. NICU Practical — เตรียมรับทารกจาก Abnormal AFV
10.1 Oligohydramnios Neonates
Pulmonary hypoplasia: เตรียม high-pressure ventilation; iNO สำหรับ PPHN component; หากต้องการ oscillator; consult pediatric pulm/surgery ก่อนคลอดถ้าทราบล่วงหน้า
Renal agenesis/urinary anomaly: expect oliguria → anuria; bilateral renal agenesis: lethal — comfort care discussion ก่อนคลอด; unilateral: survives with compensation
IUGR + AEDV/REDV: NEC 4x risk → trophic HM; GIR 6–8; hypothermia prevention; Doppler follow post-delivery (SMA velocity)
PPROM <26 wk survivor: extreme prematurity complications; ↑ infection risk (chorioamnionitis); lung immaturity — expect severe RDS despite steroids if pulmonary hypoplasia
Posterior urethral valves (if not relieved in utero): urologist on team from delivery; catheter; renal function trend; may need surgical decompression early neonatal period
10.2 Polyhydramnios Neonates
Esophageal atresia/TEF: NPO; Replogle tube; avoid mask ventilation if TEF present; surgery within hours to days
Duodenal atresia (double bubble): NGT decompression; IV fluids; surgery semi-urgent (not emergency) — trisomy 21 in 30%
Macrosomia from maternal DM: glucose monitoring early; shoulder dystocia risk at delivery
Preterm from uterine overdistension: RDS management; cord prolapse at delivery — be ready

Idiopathic polyhydramnios (normal prenatal anatomy): inspect GI carefully postnatally — 11/118 had postnatal GI anomalies not seen prenatally (Abele 2012); check first feed/bowel; observe for respiratory distress + hypoglycemia
11. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — SDP ไม่ใช่ AFI: Less Intervention, Same Outcome
AFI over-diagnoses oligohydramnios 2x vs SDP → doubles inductions + CS for fetal distress (30% vs 15%, 13% vs 7%) with NO improvement in perinatal outcome (Magann 2004; Cochrane 2009). ถ้า OB report ระบุ AFI <5 แต่ anatomy + Doppler ปกติ — ให้รู้ว่า SDP อาจยังปกติ. Communication ระหว่าง team สำคัญ: ใช้ technique อะไร → ส่งผลต่อ preterm delivery decision ที่เราต้องรับ.

PEARL 2 — Oligohydramnios 16–26 wk + SDP <2 cm = Pulmonary Hypoplasia 70%
Window of vulnerability: canalicular phase (16–26 wk). SDP <2 cm ระยะนี้ → survival 30%; SDP ≥2 cm → 98%. Probability of pulmonary hypoplasia ↑ steeply เมื่อ PPROM ก่อน 25 wk. เตรียมทีม: high-pressure ventilation, iNO for PPHN; consult parents ล่วงหน้า. Lung area ratio <66% หรือ thoracic/abdominal ratio <0.08 → virtually 100% lethal pulmonary hypoplasia (Vintzileos 1989; D'Alton 1992).

PEARL 3 — Indomethacin for Polyhydramnios: STOP ก่อน 32 wk เสมอ
Risk ductal constriction: 5% at 27 wk → ~50% at 32 wk. Chronic ductal closure → fetal hydrops + persistent pulmonary hypertension นาน. Rule: stop before 32 wk; total dose <200 mg/day; duration <48h reduces risk. เฝ้าระวัง: ductus constriction, neonatal PPHN, renal dysfunction, NEC. ถ้าทารกที่รับเข้า NICU มารดาเคยได้ indomethacin ช่วงปลาย pregnancy → check ductal flow + renal function ทันที.

PEARL 4 — Idiopathic Polyhydramnios ≠ No Postnatal Anomaly
'Normal prenatal anatomy' ≠ guaranteed. Abele 2012: 11/118 pregnancies with polyhydramnios + normal anatomy scan, normal GCT, normal TORCH → had postnatal abnormality (majority: GI atresia). ดังนั้น: ทารกจาก polyhydramnios → (1) ตรวจ oral passage ก่อน feed; (2) observe first feed; (3) early NG/Replogle ถ้าสงสัย; (4) respiratory distress + hypoglycemia risk แม้ไม่มี anomaly. Do not reassure family จนกว่าจะ rule out GI patency.

PEARL 5 — AFV เป็น Indirect Mirror ของ Fetal Renal + Placental + GI Function
Oligohydramnios: think (1) PROM, (2) renal problem, (3) uteroplacental insufficiency, (4) medication. Polyhydramnios: think (1) swallowing defect (GI anomaly), (2) diabetes, (3) chromosomal, (4) hydrops, (5) idiopathic. รู้ etiology → รู้ว่าทารกจะมี complication อะไร → เตรียม team ล่วงหน้า. AFV ไม่ใช่แค่ obstetric finding — มันเป็น prenatal telegraph ที่บอก neonatologist ว่าต้องเตรียมอะไร.
12. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Chamberlain PF et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 1984;150:245: PNM rate 11% with oligohydramnios (SDP-defined) vs 0.2% normal AFV; borderline SDP 1–2 cm: 3.7% PNM — foundational outcome data
Phelan JP et al J Reprod Med 1987;32:601: original description of AFI technique; reference ranges across gestation
Moore TR & Cayle JE Am J Obstet Gynecol 1990;162:1168: AFI normograms 16–42 wk (n=791 normal pregnancies); 5th/95th percentile reference data

Magann EF et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 2000;182:1581: AFI, SDP, two-diameter pocket in normal pregnancy: prevalence oligohydramnios differed significantly by technique (8%, 1%, 30%); no outcome assessment
Chauhan SP et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 2004;191:661 (RCT): 1080 women; AFI identified oligohydramnios 17% vs SDP 10% (P=.002); NO difference in delivery mode, NICU admission, Apgar, pH

Magann EF et al Obstet Gynecol 2004;104:5 (RCT): AFI 2x more oligohydramnios than SDP (38% vs 17%); 2x more inductions (30% vs 15%); 2x more CS fetal distress (13% vs 7%); NO improvement perinatal outcome
Nabhan AF & Abdelmoula YA Int J Gynaecol Obstet 2009;104:184 (Meta-analysis): AFI vs SDP: neither superior; AFI overdiagnoses clinically insignificant oligohydramnios → SDP preferred
Nabhan AF & Abdelmoula YA Cochrane 2008 CD006593: SDP superior for antenatal testing: AFI → more interventions without improving perinatal outcome
Magann EF et al Obstet Gynecol 2001;98:71: color Doppler over-diagnosed oligohydramnios in 37% of women with normal dye-determined AFV — does NOT improve diagnostic accuracy
Ounpraseuth ST et al J Obstet Gynaecol Res 2017;43:1122: 1190 normal AFVs; quantile regression = preferred statistical method for GA-specific reference intervals
Lee SE et al Obstet Gynecol 2007;109:634: AmniSure PAMG-1: sensitivity 98.7%, specificity 87.5% for PROM diagnosis
Vergani P et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 1994;170:1359: risk factors pulmonary hypoplasia after 2nd-trimester PPROM; probability curve by GA at rupture; SDP <2 cm = 30% survival vs ≥2 cm = 98%
D'Alton M et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 1992;166:658: thoracic/abdominal circumference ratio <0.08 in severe oligohydramnios → 100% predictive lethal pulmonary hypoplasia
Vintzileos AM et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 1989;161:606: lung area ratio: sensitivity 85%, specificity 85%; 3D lung volume sensitivity 94%/specificity 82%
Locatelli A et al Am J Obstet Gynecol 2000;183:878: serial amnioinfusion PPROM <26 wk (n=49): maintaining SDP >2 cm → ↓ pulmonary hypoplasia 62% to 10%; no abnormal neurologic outcomes
Magann EF et al Obstet Gynecol 2003;101:1261: maternal hydration (IV vs oral) equally effective for idiopathic oligohydramnios; effect short-lived
Abele H et al Fetal Diagn Ther 2012;32:251: 118 pregnancies with polyhydramnios + normal anatomy + normal GCT + normal TORCH: 11/118 had postnatal abnormalities, majority GI atresia
Hughes DS et al J Ultrasound Med 2020;39:373: accuracy AFI vs SDP to identify actual (dye-determined) polyhydramnios: AFI had higher PPV (55% vs 31%); SDP missed 5.5x more high-volume cases
Hernandez JS et al Obstet Gynecol 2012;120:759: 1951 twin pregnancies; polyhydramnios 18%; severe polyhydramnios → 20% anomaly rate; stillbirth 27% in severe monochorionic (P<.001)
13. Self-Check Q&A
Q: ทำไม SDP ถึงได้รับการแนะนำมากกว่า AFI สำหรับ antenatal testing?

ตอบ: AFI over-diagnoses oligohydramnios 2x vs SDP (38% vs 17%; 17% vs 10%) → 2x ↑ inductions, 2x ↑ CS for fetal distress — โดยไม่มี improvement ใน perinatal outcome เลย (Magann 2004; Chauhan 2004). Cochrane 2009: SDP preferred for antenatal testing. Both techniques poor at identifying actual abnormal volumes vs dye-dilution gold standard (accuracy 65–70%).
Q: Oligohydramnios ใน singleton ควร workup อะไรตามลำดับ?

ตอบ: (1) Rule out PROM: speculum, nitrazine, ferning, AmniSure — PROM เป็น 50% ของสาเหตุ. (2) Fetal anatomy: renal/urinary system (renal agenesis = Doppler RA), bladder morphology. (3) Fetal growth + Doppler: UA Doppler (AEDV/REDV = high risk). (4) Pulmonary hypoplasia assessment: ถ้า GA 16–26 wk — SDP ≥2 cm → 98% survival.
Q: Bilateral renal agenesis: ทำไม US อาจเห็น 'kidneys' ทั้งที่ไม่มี?

ตอบ: Fetal adrenals hypertrophy เมื่อไม่มี kidneys → อาจ mimic renal structures บน US. การยืนยัน: Doppler imaging เพื่อ document absence of renal arteries จาก aortic bifurcation — ไม่มี renal arteries = renal agenesis confirmed. สิ่งที่ต้องรู้: virtual anhydramnios from 16 wk; bilateral = lethal → comfort care discussion ก่อนคลอด.
Q: Indomethacin ใน polyhydramnios: mechanism, dose, เมื่อไหร่ต้อง stop?

ตอบ: Mechanism: ↑ fetal vasopressin → ↓ fetal urine (antidiuretic) + ↑ fluid reabsorption ใน fetal lung + ↑ transmembranous absorption. Dose: 25 mg oral q6h (up to 100 mg q4–8h). Stop เมื่อ AFV ↓ 1/3–1/2 หรือ AFI <20 cm. MUST stop before 32 wk: ductal constriction risk 5% at 27 wk → 50% at 32 wk. Total dose <200 mg/day + duration <48h ↓ complications.
Q: Pulmonary hypoplasia: window of vulnerability คือเมื่อไหร่ และ sonographic predictors มีอะไรบ้าง?

ตอบ: Window: 16–26 wk (canalicular phase). SDP <2 cm → 30% survival; SDP ≥2 cm → 98% survival (Vergani 1994). Sonographic predictors: (1) lung area ratio = (chest − cardiac)/chest × 100; normal >66%; sensitivity 85%, specificity 85% (Vintzileos 1989); (2) thoracic/abdominal circumference ratio <0.08 → 100% lethal pulmonary hypoplasia (D'Alton 1992); (3) 3D lung volume: sensitivity 94%.
Q: AmniSure คืออะไร และใช้ในกรณีใด?

ตอบ: AmniSure: monoclonal antibody ต่อ PAMG-1 (placental alpha microglobulin-1) ซึ่งมีใน AF เข้มข้นกว่าใน cervicovaginal secretions 100,000x. ใช้เมื่อ: PROM ไม่ชัดเจนจาก pooling/nitrazine/ferning. Sensitivity 98.7%, specificity 87.5%. False-positive: มีเลือดปน. False-negative: ตรวจ >12h หลัง suspected rupture. ดีกว่า ferning/nitrazine ในกรณีที่ equivocal.
Q: ทารกจาก idiopathic polyhydramnios ที่ anatomy US ปกติ: ควร observe อะไร postnatally?

ตอบ: ตรวจ oral passage (OG tube ผ่านหรือไม่); observe first feed (regurgitation/choking → TEF/EA); ดู bowel opening. 11/118 pregnancies มี postnatal GI anomaly ที่ไม่เห็น prenatally (Abele 2012) — ส่วนใหญ่ GI atresia. Monitor respiratory distress + hypoglycemia (occur even without anomaly). Do NOT reassure ครอบครัวจนกว่า GI patency ชัดเจน.
Q: TTTS: sonographic signs คืออะไร และ surveillance ควรเป็นอย่างไร?

ตอบ: Stuck twin: donor fetus 'glued' ติด uterine wall — ไม่เห็น intervening membrane หรือ membrane เกาะ fetus tight like plastic wrap (TOPS/TTTS sign). Recipient: polyhydramnios; donor: oligohydramnios. Surveillance: every 2 wk from 2nd trimester ถ้า discordant; every 2 wk for concordant MC/DA. TTTS: 3–5x ↑ mortality/morbidity; refer MFM; laser therapy for MC/DA TTTS.
Q: ACE inhibitors ใน pregnancy: ทำไมถึง contraindicated และ neonatal effects คืออะไร?

ตอบ: Mechanism: severe fetal hypotension → ↓ renal blood flow → oligohydramnios + neonatal anuria. Neonatal effects: prolonged anuria, renal anomalies, skull ossification defects (calvaria underdevelopment), death. CONTRAINDICATED throughout all trimesters of pregnancy. ถ้าทารก expose in utero to ACE inhibitors → monitor: renal function (creatinine/BUN), UO, BUN/Cr; cranial US for cranial defects; BP support PRN.

Day 121 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.24
บทต่อไป: Chapter 24 — Perinatal Infections and Chorioamnionitis. เตรียมตัว: epidemiology ของ chorioamnionitis; clinical vs histologic chorioamnionitis; intrauterine inflammation pathway → preterm birth; neonatal effects (EOS, FIRS — fetal inflammatory response syndrome); antibiotic management (ampicillin + gentamicin protocol); long-term neonatal neurodevelopment after chorioamnionitis; specific perinatal pathogens (GBS, Listeria, E. coli, TORCH in context of infection).
